title: ⚔️ Chapter Boss ⭐⭐⭐: Advanced Phrasal and Prepositional Verbs

questions.prompt: Find the sentence that contains an error in particle use.

options.text: We came up against fierce opposition from local residents.
She has always looked up to her mentor.
I cannot go along on their proposal — it makes no sense.
They ran out of funding halfway through the project.

questions.explanation: The error is in the third option: the correct verb is go along with (agree/accept), not go along on. The particle is always with. The remaining three sentences all use came up against, looked up to, and ran out of correctly.

questions.prompt: Complete: "The board voted to ___ the outdated dress code that had been in place for decades."
questions.explanation: Do away with means to abolish or eliminate, which fits voting to remove an outdated rule. Put up with means to tolerate (the opposite intention), come up against means to encounter an obstacle, and get on with means to continue doing something or have a good relationship — none carry the meaning of abolition.

questions.prompt: Complete: "After years of denial, she finally ___ the fact that she needed help."
questions.explanation: Face up to means to accept and deal honestly with something difficult. It is a fixed three-part verb: face + up + to + object. Face up on, face out with, and face away from are not real verbs. The particle to is inseparable from the combination.

questions.prompt:
Read: "The contractor had been overcharging clients for years and, remarkably, had ___ it until a whistleblower came forward."
Choose the correct multi-word verb.
questions.explanation: Get away with means to do something wrong and escape punishment — exactly what the contractor did until exposure. Put up with means to tolerate (the clients' perspective, not the contractor's). Gone along with means agreed to, and looked down on means regarded as inferior — neither fits the idea of escaping undetected.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a separable phrasal verb with a noun object and moves the object between verb and particle?

options.text: She looked the new proposal into.
They put the meeting off until next week.
He put up the noise with stoically.
We came the problem against up.

questions.explanation: Put off is a separable phrasal verb, and a noun object may go between verb and particle or after the particle: put the meeting off or put off the meeting — both are correct. Look into and put up with are inseparable, so their objects cannot be inserted mid-verb. The fourth option scrambles the particles entirely.

questions.prompt: Complete the formal sentence: "The new legislation is designed to ___ the tax loopholes that large corporations have exploited for decades."
questions.explanation: Do away with (abolish/eliminate) is the correct choice — the legislation is designed to eliminate tax loopholes. Run out of means to exhaust a supply, get away with means to escape punishment, and put up with means to tolerate. The slightly formal register of do away with also suits this legal/political context.
